sentences.text: Yes.
Compared what preceded, you know, we got a little bit more food, not that it would be something where you would have to keep a diet.
Also, the hygienic conditions were a little bit better.
And being young, you simply shed the memory of the horror of Auschwitz relatively quickly.
Like, for instance, we really, every morning, were permitted to wash, not that there would be any hot water.
But the winter was not so drastic, and the-- so that we had enough water.
And we were warned that there will be bombardments of the factory, that we will have to be very quick to evacuate, which will be permitted.
We were not permitted to go to the shelters like the civilian population.
Also, the latrines were-- simply saw that they were kept clean.
And we were only men.

You know, I forgot to mention in Auschwitz that that was one of the great humiliations, which in the end, somehow culminated in indifference, for there were a huge space of cement, kind of huge blocks with round holes in it, where were sitting men and women together.
And then later somebody achieved even that they put some kind of a divider, the jute bags, so that you didn't see the face always, but you just see the rest of the body.
But at that point certainly, nobody minded, for we had been so decimated.
So-- I call it dehumanized-- that simply didn't matter.
Here it was a clean proposition.
I mentioned there how we started to wash the laundry for the SS men, which kept many of us alive, for I succeeded always to get something for it.
Or when I succeeded to smuggle things in the laundry, which we reorganized somewhere, either on a block or in a kitchen, especially in the SS kitchen.
And when I look back, I certainly-- to feed myself was very simple.
But to feed 15 people I had to be very kind of-- not necessarily courageous, but inventive.
So that I sometimes arrived with plenty of goodies, which we shared.
Hm?
Inventive.
I am being called by the SS responsible for the SS kitchen, the head of the SS kitchen.

I had to go either through the main gate, where it would be an SS man who leads me through, or I could be permitted to go through a small gate, which connects the SS part of the camp with the prisoners' camp.
Now, to pass through the gate, I have to pass two watchtowers with SS and machine guns in it.
As I approach simply to explain them what I am going to do, that I am going to bring the laundry, so the guy on the tower says, wash my laundry.
I said, sure.
But make it snappy, for if you want to have it back before they change you, so I need to have it within literally 15 minutes when I am coming back.
So they both leave me their little bag with laundry at the gate.
And I go.
And in the kitchen work two check prisoners who enter the laundry put two whole margarines, whole cubes of margarines, half a dozen eggs, piece of salami-- something totally unheard of.
And I quietly will carry through, take these two little bundles of these two SS men, and I am already in the camp.
Nobody checks me, naturally for it.
That is, I mean, as we say, one hand washes the other hand.
I gave them their laundry, and I am left in peace.
It becomes a kind of a deadly game, but a game which feeds people.
A little funnier story.
The conditions were so that people were dying, really.
My comrades were dying of malnutrition and terrible, horrible, absolutely horrendous.
They kept there for nine hours, you know.
And let's say carrying cement bags or working there.
They are rebuilding.
In fact, it was something.
The mortality that was really bad.
People didn't die, but people were so weak that we had to rotate them.
There was some kind of a primitive lazaretto, and the head of the lazaretto was also a German prison-- I mean, Aryan German prisoner-- who was in the camp, for he was gay.
So-- but something human was left in him in spite of his long imprisonment and really torture he went through, so that we, always-- the sickest we kept in this kind of lazaretto.
And so we tried simply to rotate the people so that they would survive as long as they could.
And so it happened-- two things happened to me, which were quite significant for my existence there.
One of them was that we had the Romanian SS-- I mean, Romanian/German.
Volks German, V-O-L-K-S, meaning of the folk, of the people, of the German origin-- who was especially stupid.
His name was Romejko, R-O-M-E-J-K-O.
And he was a bully.
Oh, not that he would kill anybody, but he liked really to smack people and to kick people.
And we had-- we-- we got him tamed.
And he liked to go actually to the laundry, for we were inside of the laundry, he didn't have to pay any attention to anything.
So he sat there and smoked a pipe.
And when it came to the lunch break, so we have been sitting in a kind of a half circle, and in front or me there are two SS men who are eating salami and bread and drinking beer to it.
And we had their soup or something.
And Romejko, would start being talkative, and he would say, so what can you-- what can you do, you bums?
I am translating from German.
Using the word schlawiner, it's-- means 'bums.' Nothing too bad.
For an SS man, not too bad.
And we were so trained that, I remember we have been eight at that point, and in unison-- we trained it-- in unison we said to him, Nix, Herr Oberscharfuhrer.
Nothing, Herr Oberscharfuhrer.
O-B-E-R-S-C-H-A-R-F-U with an-H-R-E-R.
Nix, Herr Obserscharfuhrer.
And he says, eh, I?
He call Lesen-- Lesen, L-E-S-E-N.
The N he didn't pronounce.
[INAUDIBLE] Schreiben, S-C-H-R-E-I-B-E-N he didn't pronounce.
I can even ride the horses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What can-- what can you do, your bums?
Nothing, Herr Obescharffhrer.
And then he took another bite and he looked at us, kind of a glorious look, and he would say, I can also ride a bike, you bums.
I am an artist.
I am an artist of thousand arts, a thousand kunstler, an artist of thousand arts.
And as he made the break, so we gave him a hand.
And we put him into  a situation that he didn't know really how to react.
And so it happened that he never hit, never beat anyone of my commando.
We had him tamed.
And it was always-- I mean, it repeat itself.
Every second week, we got Romejko, Officer Romejko.
We got the same story, and we got the same peace.
And then it was end of 1944 when there was-- came a new SS man.
He was a youngish guy who had a little trace of French in his German.
He was from [INAUDIBLE].
And he was one of the types like this SS man [INAUDIBLE] in Auschwitz.
He would simply leave us-- lead us out without counting, without nothing, no signature, no nothing, no report.
He didn't want to have anything reported.
And the first time we were on the way to the laundry, so he said to me, what is actually the best bartering commodity one can give you?
So I said, Officer, why would you give us anything?
He says, oh, for I feel like it.
Not everyone has to be like the rest you are accustomed to.
So just to test him I said, the best bartering commodities are cigarettes.
We can buy anything for cigarettes.
Just to see what will happen.
The man disappeared, but came back to the laundry in about half an hour and gave me a box of 200 cigarettes. 200 cigarettes?
It's like if you would get-- I don't know-- a car.
It was an unbelievable-- unbelievable fortune.
For three cigarettes, one could buy half a loaf of bread from the civilian prisoners.
And I said to him, Officer, first of all, I don't know how to thank you.
I have no means.
We have no means to repay anything.
And beside that, how do we get it into the camp?
He said, number one, you can hide it here and use it.
And if you want to bring it into the camp, then, I mean, bring me some of your guys who will be in the first row when we are marching back.
And he apologized to the five guys, and he said, I am awfully sorry, but one of you will get two smacks into the face from me, for I have to make it real McCoy.
And as we were marching in, so he stopped the SS men and said, these are my Jews, and I will search them.
And as it was a little too slow for him, so one of them got two smacks.
And that was enough.
We were in.
And then we repeated minimally three, four times.
And then he came with a proposition.
What would happen-- no, no.
First of all, I hear that you are married, that you got married in the camp.
Crazy enough of you.
But is your wife alive?
And I said, Officer, do I know?
I know that she was young and she passed through the selection.
And she can be only in one of the three camps, main camps.
One of them G-R-O-S-S, capital R-O-S-E-N, Gross-Rosen; the other one is Neugamme, capital N-E-U-G-A-M-M-E.
Or the other one Stutthoff, S-T-U-T-T-H-O-F-F.
She might be on the same small commando as I am, if she is alive.
And he says, eh, let's find out.
Write me three letters.
I'll give you the paper.
Do you have a pen?
I'll give you a pen.
I got paper and pen.
And I was lying there, and I was thinking, the guy is an agent provocateur.
I'll be hanged.
Then I said to myself, if he wants to have me that-- have me killed or have me dead, he just can do it.
He can shoot me on the spot, do absolutely anything.
That is recourse, no remorse, no nothing.
I mean, the guy might be crazy, that's a different story.
But what can happen?
I can only lose one life.
I wrote three letters and gave it to him.
And Eva will tell you, so it happened that an SS man appeared at the barbed wire of the camp where she was south of Hamburg at that point and gave her the letter.
And the other two letters were delivered into the main camps.
They were interesting, and I spoke to people who got them.
And she was able to write an answer, which I did not get, for we already moved out of the camp at that point.
But we had an address where she was [INAUDIBLE] in Prague where it could be delivered.
And when I was liberated and went there to search whether there is any sign of life, I found the letter.
That was this SS man.
I blame myself, but I forgot his name.
I remembered his name.
It was after the war.
As a vignette, we are already liberated.
I am in Prague.
We are standing in a queue for food for the Czechs.
They went absolutely berserk to give us something, so they-- the-- the various housewives, they were cooking.
I mean, preparing soap and food stuff for us.
And as we are standing in the queue, we see there an SS man, our SS man.
No, no, no, no.
A bad one.
So we started to yell.
We are starting to pursue him.
And anyhow, a Russian got-- a Russian patrol caught him.
They took us to the military barracks in Prague, and they, after an interrogation and investigation, they shot him in front of us.

Very kind of satisfactory tremendously unpleasant feeling, for it was after the war and it should have been handled in-- but the Russian officer asked us if I-- we could name somebody who were fair Germans.
And I was able to give them three names.
One of them was this SS man.
I don't know whether he was caught or whether he stayed alive or whatever.
And one of them was this Mrs.
Ruhl from the laundry.
And she became a mayor of Ruhland in the East-- in East Germany.
Just I sidetracked that I don't know what happened to him.
Now, in the-- when it came to bombardment, we were warned.
The Allied threw leaflets that it will be bombed.
At the beginning, you know, when we came to the camp and we have seen this hundreds and hundreds of planes, I mean, over us, we were absolutely thrilled and jubilant.
And then simply the last moment we were told, not even by five, run, run, run.
And we were running out of the camp.
And they took us into-- there were coal mines where they were getting the brown coal for the manufacture of the gas.
And it was about hundred meters going down.
And it was sand, and we just slid down on our [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] backside.
Simply slid down, and we were permitted to enter the adits of the mines.
We are just [INAUDIBLE] the thing.
Shaking, shaking.
And when we came back, it was an absolute bedlam.
I have seen the engine, a locomotive, a train engine sitting in the third floor in the factory.
There were these huge bombs.
Smashed to pieces.
Then they decided-- the camp wasn't touched too much.
Then they decided we should build a trench in the camp so that we can hide there.
So there were specifications, so we dug the trench.
And we covered it with boards, and then we should cover it with half a meter of sand.
And there came about four lorries, big trucks, with sand.
And they-- the sand was caked, so they brought sieves, and they brought spades.
And there was a commando created, which was called Sand Commando.
They brought it in October, 1944.
And we left the camp in April-- 18 of April, 1945.
And the Sand Commando was active.
We were sifting the sand from one corner of the camp to the other corner.
Just sifting the sand.
The sand never came on this things, for it was registered as Sand Commando that we are just fooling.
And nobody found out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, in spring-- no, in early, early spring, 1945, they took 300 of our guys and sent them away.
I found later that they were sick, and they were sent to Bergen-Belsen.
And then, to replace them, we got there are some Aryan Poles, and then we got some Aryan Ukrainians who were in the camp of reason probably that they have been a little fair.
There was never-- there was, I would say, a couple of hundred of them.
We were originally a thousand.
And-- and--
1,000 men who came with the transport from Auschwitz.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the bombardments were practically every second week.
And it took some toll in the camp, for it was a tremendously difficult.
They-- they didn't evacuate us in the end, so that the terrain there was sandy so we dug just a little kind of holes.
And when there was an air raid, so we just lied in it and hoped that we don't have a direct hit.
A few times I flew through the air, just the pressure, simply.
But about only six, seven prisoners were killed.
Otherwise, I mean, it was quite OK.
We all hoped.
You know, we had all the news, for, I mean, in the factory, one had the-- the prisoners, especially the Americans, and the French.
They knew.
They had the-- they had the news.
And the three German-- our-- our heads, the-- which I mentioned, one of them was so-called the eldest prisoner of the camp.
His name was Kapelke, K-A-P-E-L-K-E.
Extremely fair guy.
So they had the permission.
They were connected to the SS.
They listened to the broadcast, the German broadcast, so that I listened.
And I have heard that, for instance, our heroic real troops came into contact with the front lines of the enemy, and the city often.
We have seen how the-- the-- the front is moving closer towards us.
And then came-- the day was 18 of April, 1945.
And for a day we could hear canons.
And on that morning, we started to hear machine guns.
And there was an absolute bedlam in the camp.
And the SS men simply brought from somewhere two wagons, and they threw in there whatever they have got.
And one wagon was empty, and we got-- I mean, people hitched into it, and we started to march out of the camp.
We started the famous march, death march.
And--
The first day it was not bad.
It was-- it was-- I say we were lucky in two senses, for two [INAUDIBLE] really crucial, crucial winters.
The winters were not bad.
And we were told we are going-- I got it out of this SS man that we are going to Mauthausen.
Mauthausen.
And where are we?
It was a question of 700 kilometers, or 500 kilometers.
And they marched as was tremendous speed so that the first day, we had marched about 42 kilometers.
And if you didn't have good shoes, something on, it was like a living catastrophe.
The first day, the SS were pretty-- they were pretty panicky, so they didn't do too much to us.
We slept the first night in a-- a factory which was manufacturing bricks.
And then early in the morning, out, out, starting to walk.
We had practically nothing to eat.
We were permitted to stop and to drink, and then it started to rain, so with the water, it wasn't so bad.
And then on the track, simply as we were marching, so there were thousands and thousands of Germans on the-- on the road.
They were trying to escape from the Russians towards the Americans or towards the English.
And they were clogging the road, and the SS simply were yelling and screaming so they would let us through.
And then to this came the Russian low-lying airplanes were strafing, I mean, whatever was there.
So while the Germans were running away from their procession, so we were ordered to run towards the procession and steal whatever we can steal, for they didn't have anything to eat.
So we stole for them.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we are successful, so we swallowed something, or we hid something.
So this is how we kept ourself alive.
I have got the route.
We did it, I mean, I did it in 1995.
Last year we-- I retraced it.
It was 350 kilometers.
From 18 of April to the 1 of May.
We came to a city called Varnsdorf, which was already on the Czech territory, former Czech territory.
It was Sudetenland.
It was part of Germany.
And then they let us stay in a evacuated textile factory.
When the SS found out that Hitler committed suicide, and-- that still didn't stop them to hang people.
And whoever, I mean, we were suspected that they wanted to escape.
And then they split us.
They took about 250 Aryans who were in the camp, they took them away.
And we stayed there.
And this had-- the German head, Kapelke, persuaded them that-- listen now the logic what they used.
If you leave the prisoners, or if you shoot them, so what will happen?
Most probably the war is lost, though the SS men protested.
We have got a secret weapon.
The Fuhrer-- he says, there is no Fuhrer.
Don't depend on secret weapon.
If you kill the Jews, you will be killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the other hand, you need the Jews, for if you don't have the Jews, you have to go and fight the Russians.
You still are trained soldiers.
You've got the weapon and ammunition.
Put the Jews on the train and let them take them to the nearest camp.
Don't try to kill them.
They followed.
They put us on wagons, flat coal wagons.
There were not so many.
I mean, there might have been 40, 50 in one wagon.
And they put an engine on, and as they start to drag us, we found out that we are not going to Mauthausen, occupied by the American.
Dachau, occupied by the American.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Naturally, Sachsenhausen was occupied by that time by the Russians.
They are taking us to Theresienstadt.
Part of the Theresienstadt was a very bad fortress, simply where there was the toughest political prisoners.
So that's where they will take us.
We stopped after a day and stood on the [INAUDIBLE], which was formally a Czech territory.
There was the train, and the-- the city called Ceska Lipa, C-E-S-K-A capital L-I-P-A, was on a kind of a hills.
And the civil-- civilian population looked at us.
OK.
